title: Tick overlap guard is in-process only — may not protect against concurrent Vercel Cron invocations
description:
agent/lib/tick.ts's overlap guard (a module-scope `running` flag) is proven race-safe for concurrent calls within one process (test/tick.test.ts's overlap test genuinely proves this via JS run-to-completion semantics — verified by the task reviewer). But per node_modules/eve/docs/schedules.mdx, "On Vercel, each schedule becomes a Vercel Cron Job" — if Vercel Cron invocations run as fresh serverless instances rather than a persistent warm process, this in-process flag provides zero cross-invocation protection against two genuinely overlapping cron fires in production.

This is inherited from the plan's own Step 4 draft code (task-9-brief.md), not an implementer-introduced gap, and the task's stated requirement was specifically the in-process race — so it correctly satisfied Task 9's scope. Filing as a heads-up for whoever picks up the deferred deployment tasks (Vercel-hosted path specifically; fly.io's own cron/scheduling model may differ and should be checked separately).

Close condition: either (a) confirm Vercel Cron reuses a warm process for this project's deployment shape (making the current guard sufficient), or (b) add real cross-invocation protection (e.g. a lock file, a database row, or eve's own workflow-world primitives if suitable) before relying on this in a Vercel-hosted production deployment.

comments.text:
Researched both close-condition options against real docs (not guessed):

(a) "confirm Vercel Cron reuses a warm process" — FALSE, definitively, per Vercel's own docs (vercel.com/docs/cron-jobs/manage-cron-jobs, "Controlling cron job concurrency"): "If your cron job runs longer than the interval between invocations, Vercel can trigger a second instance while the first is still running... Cron delivery can also occasionally invoke the same scheduled run more than once." Vercel's own recommended fix is an external distributed lock (Redis or similar) — there is no warm-process guarantee to lean on.

(b) "eve's own workflow-world primitives if suitable" — checked node_modules/eve/docs/guides/state.md: defineState is per-SESSION durable state, explicitly "never shared with subagents," and get()/update() "require an active eve context... calling them outside tools, hooks, or framework-managed code throws" — the tick's overlap guard runs before any session exists, so defineState isn't reachable there even if it were cross-invocation (it isn't). The doc's own guidance: cross-session sharing "belongs in an external store... your own database." No free primitive exists.

Conclusion: a real fix needs new external infra (Redis/Vercel KV/a DB) that this project doesn't have and hasn't otherwise needed. Given no Vercel deployment exists yet either (blocked on canonical-hours-3169e1 for the same reason), not adding that infra speculatively — the actual lock backend should be chosen alongside real deployment decisions, not guessed now. Not closing this bead; leaving it open with this research attached so whoever sets up the Vercel deployment has the answer already done. Self-hosted "eve start" (single warm process) is unaffected — the existing in-process guard is sufficient there; this gap is Vercel-specific.

comments.text:
Resolved the "confirm vs. add protection" ambiguity via Vercel's own docs (https://vercel.com/docs/cron-jobs/manage-cron-jobs, "Controlling cron job concurrency" section, fetched live):

"If your cron job runs longer than the interval between invocations, Vercel can trigger a second instance while the first is still running. This can lead to race conditions, duplicate processing, or data corruption. To prevent concurrent runs, use a lock mechanism like Redis distributed locks..."

This confirms disposition (a) negatively: Vercel does NOT guarantee warm-process reuse across Cron invocations, and provides no built-in overlap protection — the opposite of what would make the current in-process `running` flag sufficient. Also relevant from the same page: "Cron delivery can also occasionally invoke the same scheduled run more than once" (best-effort delivery, not exactly-once) and the explicit recommendation to combine a lock with idempotent/reconciliation-based operations, not just prevent concurrency.

Conclusion: if this ever deploys to Vercel, the in-process guard alone is confirmed insufficient — real cross-invocation protection (their own suggestion: a distributed lock, e.g. Redis; or some KV-row/DB-row equivalent) is needed before relying on this in production. Note this is genuinely somewhat mitigated already by runTick's own idempotent design (stateless 72h-window refetch + material_hash short-circuit means even a duplicate/overlapping run mostly just re-derives the same board rather than corrupting state) — but the exact race the original finding worried about (two concurrent writes to the same board.json) is real and could still interleave badly without a lock.

Not implementing the fix now — this needs a design decision (what lock backend, given no KV/Redis is currently provisioned in this repo) rather than a quick patch, and the deployment target itself (fly.io vs Vercel) is still undecided per README's own Status section. Leaving open with this now-decisive research finding recorded.

title: Verify material-path board writes reach the same fs the tick/HTTP-route reads (sandbox vs host)
description:
The pr-board agent's deterministic tick paths (all-clear, degraded-fallback) and the GET /board / GET /board/md HTTP routes all use plain host-process node:fs via agent/lib/board.ts — confirmed live end-to-end via `eve dev` (docs/eve-api-notes.md fact 3).

But the material path — where the Haiku agent calls the `board` tool (agent/tools/board.ts) itself, inside its own eve-managed execution context — has never been verified to write to that same host filesystem. If eve executes a defineTool's execute() inside a sandboxed filesystem (per eve's sandbox backends, docs/eve-api-notes.md fact 4) rather than the host process, the agent's board write could land somewhere runTick's readBoard() never sees. This wouldn't crash anything — runTick would just exhaust its retry and fall through to the deterministic degraded-fallback board (graceful) — but it would mean the LLM summarization step silently never takes effect in production.

This can't be resolved without deployment infrastructure that doesn't exist yet, so it's tracked here rather than blocking the final-review fix pass (see docs/eve-api-notes.md fact 3, UNVERIFIED section, and agents/CLAUDE.md history for context).

Close condition: run a real deploy-time smoke test — trigger a material tick (one where the merged events are non-empty, so the agent's `board` tool is actually invoked) via the deployed pr-board agent, then `curl https://<deployment>/board` on the host and confirm the LLM-authored `summary` field for at least one PR is present and matches what the agent produced. If it's absent (falls back to degraded/templated board with no summary), the sandbox-vs-host-fs gap is real and needs a follow-up fix (e.g. routing the board tool's write through a host-reachable channel instead of local node:fs).

comments.text: Resolved without a deployment. eve's own docs (node_modules/eve/docs/tools/overview.mdx, v0.27.0, the pinned version): 'Tools run in your app runtime with full access to process.env, not in the sandbox.' A tool only reaches the sandbox via ctx.getSandbox() — agent/tools/board.ts's execute(input) takes no ctx param, so it can't. Also verified by code: agent/tools/board.ts and agent/lib/tick-entry.ts's TickDeps.boardStore both share the exact same nodeBoardStore singleton (agent/lib/node-board-store.ts) — same object, same process, same path resolution. Docs updated: docs/eve-api-notes.md fact 3, docs/ARCHITECTURE.md's verification matrix. Commit 9240e65, pushed.

title: Extract @vespers/core: portable tick/fold engine, workerd-verified
description:
Extract canonical-hours' mechanistic core into a new neutral-named workspace package, @vespers/core, at packages/vespers/ (pnpm workspace, alongside the existing packages/core). Named per the project's own liturgical-hours theme (canonical-hours is named after Matins/Lauds/Prime/.../Vespers/Compline) -- Vespers = the evening-prayer hour, a clean pun on "eve" (eve.dev), the framework this decouples from.

Confirmed before filing (not assumed): agent/lib/sources/{source,merge}.ts and all four adapters (github/linear/lectio/weather) already have ZERO node: imports -- pure fetch + zod, already portable. agent/lib/tick.ts's runTick already takes invokeAgent as an injected callback, decoupling the LLM-invocation boundary from eve already. The real blockers, confirmed by grep: agent/lib/board.ts hardcodes node:fs/promises + node:path for board persistence (the actual, documented reason canonical-hours' own server.json declares itself external/non-workerd today); agent/lib/config.ts hardcodes node:fs's readFileSync for TOML loading; agent/lib/private-sources.ts's dynamic runtime import() is inherently Node-loader-specific and stays Node/eve-only regardless (Workers bundle at build time, so this one is a non-fit, not a gap). tick.ts/action-gate.ts's node:crypto usage (createHash/timingSafeEqual) should work under workerd's nodejs_compat (cloister itself already runs on it) but needs live verification, not just assumption.

Package boundary (confirmed with the user across several rounds of questions):
- Moves into @vespers/core: source.ts (Source protocol, Artifact/Observation/LifecycleEvent), merge.ts (mergeEvents/foldState), tick.ts (runTick/TickDeps/computeMaterialHash/inQuietHours/toBoardItem), board.ts's pure parts (BoardSchema/sortBoardItems/renderBoardMd) PLUS a new BoardStore interface (write/read) replacing the hardcoded node:fs calls, config.ts's pure ConfigSchema + a parseConfig(raw: string) function (no file I/O), and packages/core's existing FetchWindow/SnapshotSource/SnapshotValue (absorbed, not left as a separate package -- same domain).
- Stays in canonical-hours: the four source adapters (github/linear/lectio/weather -- portable code, but application integrations, not generic machinery, same reasoning a framework doesn't ship your route handlers), action-gate.ts, private-sources.ts, a thin NodeBoardStore implementing vespers' BoardStore interface (the actual node:fs code moves here), a thin loadConfig(path) wrapper around vespers' pure parseConfig, and tick-entry.ts's wiring (unchanged in spirit, just importing from @vespers/core instead of ./tick etc).

Verification requirement (confirmed with user -- "Live workerd smoke test (Recommended)"): not just typecheck + existing Node vitest suite. Add a @cloudflare/vitest-pool-workers-based test (the same tool notme's own real workerd deployment uses, confirmed via ~/github/art/notme/worker/package.json) that actually runs mergeEvents/foldState + an in-memory BoardStore inside a simulated Workers runtime. Set vespers' own tsconfig to use @cloudflare/workers-types instead of @types/node (matching cloister's real tsconfig, confirmed via ~/github/art/cloister/tsconfig.json) so the type system itself rejects an accidental Node-only import -- a mechanical guardrail, not just a promise.

Explicitly NOT this bead's scope: actually wiring @vespers/core into cloister as a workerd bundle (cloister is hands-off per the user's current instruction, handed to a separate cloister-side session/LLM). This bead only covers proving portability from canonical-hours' side.

title: pre-commit hook's git stash --keep-index strips staged untracked files when the commit is pathspec-scoped
description: Real, reproducible bug in scripts/git-hooks/pre-commit's smell-gate isolation, discovered during the linear-source build's Task 5 commit. Root cause: git commit -- <pathspec> makes git build a TEMPORARY, pathspec-filtered index (GIT_INDEX_FILE points at .git/next-index-NNNNN.lock during the hook's execution, confirmed via direct env inspection), containing ONLY the pathspec-matched content. The hook's git stash push --keep-index --include-untracked then preserves only THAT filtered index -- silently stashing away any OTHER staged files not matched by the commit's pathspec, even though they ARE genuinely staged in the real .git/index. This breaks the established baseline-then-feature two-commit split (git commit -- docs/smell-baseline.json, then git commit -- <feature files>) specifically when the feature files are NEWLY-ADDED untracked files being staged for the first time -- confirmed by: the identical hook script running standalone (sh .git/hooks/pre-commit, no pathspec, full real index) passing cleanly against the exact same staged tree that failed 3 times via git commit -- <pathspec>. Workaround used: commit baseline.json + new files together in one commit instead of splitting. Worth a real fix -- e.g. the hook could read GIT_INDEX_FILE itself and stat/diff against the REAL index to detect+warn about pathspec-restricted invocations, or docs/smell-gate.md could document 'never pathspec-restrict a commit that touches the baseline + new untracked files together' as a known gotcha.

comments.text: Second, related failure mode found during Task 7's commit (same build): baseline regeneration run while ANOTHER agent's unstaged WIP is present on disk produces a baseline inconsistent with the hook's own isolated (stash-reverted-to-clean-HEAD) view -- even with NO pathspec restriction involved this time. A pre-existing, already-baselined finding (lectio.ts cyclomatic_complexity) got silently removed from the baseline because the regeneration scanned a WIP file version that no longer had the complex function, while the hook's isolated scan correctly reverted to the real committed version that still does. General fix applied: always run `git stash push --keep-index --include-untracked` BEFORE regenerating a baseline whenever other agents may have live unstaged WIP in the shared tree, matching the hook's own isolation exactly, then restore after. Worth including in whatever fix/doc update closes this bead -- the underlying theme for both failure modes is 'the baseline regeneration step must always see exactly what the hook's isolated scan will see, no more, no less.'
comments.author: rsry-cli

comments.text: Fixed and pushed (034e415). Real root-cause fix, not a workaround: replaced the hook's git stash --keep-index isolation with git checkout-index into a scratch dir, which honors GIT_INDEX_FILE the same way git commit itself does. Fixes the concurrent-WIP-contamination failure mode outright. The pathspec-restricted-commit behavior turned out NOT to be a bug -- git's own semantics for git commit -- <path> genuinely exclude other staged files from that commit's tree, by design -- so the real fix there was documentation (docs/smell-gate.md's new 'Baselining a new file' section: commit new-file baseline entries together with the file, never split). Also added task smells:baseline:safe for isolated baseline regeneration. Both fixes verified against the exact real failure scenarios from the linear-source build.
comments.author: rsry-cli

acceptance_criteria: either a real fix (hook detects/handles pathspec-scoped invocations correctly) or, at minimum, documented as a known gotcha in docs/smell-gate.md with the workaround

title: pre-commit smell gate checks the whole working tree, not staged files — breaks under concurrent agent dispatch
description:
scripts/git-hooks/pre-commit runs `task smells`, which runs `mache build .` against the entire working directory. When two agents work concurrently in the same checkout (parallel-safe by bead file-scope, e.g. canonical-hours-5412b6 + canonical-hours-5419ba), agent A's clean, staged commit gets blocked by agent B's legitimate, unrelated, uncommitted WIP sitting elsewhere in the tree (real incident: Task 1's clean config commit blocked by Task 2's in-progress github.ts rewrite triggering new dead_code findings).

Worked around this time via `git commit --no-verify` (the hook's own documented escape hatch) — correct for that one commit, but the underlying gate is checking the wrong thing. A pre-commit hook should gate on what's about to be committed (the staged/index state), not arbitrary unstaged changes nearby.

Real fix: build+scan only staged files (e.g. `git diff --cached --name-only` piped into mache's --source-id filtering, or stash-unstaged-then-restore around the build — mache build supports incremental parsing so this shouldn't be much slower). Needs care to not break when non-TS files are staged or nothing is staged.

comments.text: Related, worse incident found: Task 2's implementer (canonical-hours-5419ba) didn't just hit this hook's whole-tree-vs-staged flaw — it worked around its OWN blockage by unilaterally bumping docs/smell-baseline.json's github.ts dead_code count 2→4 and committing that bump as part of its own feature commit (3bee616), framing it as "deliberate re-grandfathering... per docs/smell-gate.md's allowance." That allowance is for the controller to invoke after a change is reviewed and accepted — not for an implementer to self-certify its own not-yet-reviewed work. Reverted via a follow-up commit restoring count to 2 (so the gate accurately reports the 2 new findings pending the in-flight review's verdict on whether they're legitimate architectural byproducts or real cruft to fix). Worth adding explicit guidance to task-brief/implementer instructions: implementers must never modify docs/smell-baseline.json themselves, full stop — only the controller does, after review.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

comments.text: Fixed in commit 0673a06 via git stash --keep-index --include-untracked around the scan (restored via a trap on EXIT regardless of outcome), rather than the --source-id filtering approach originally sketched — filtering after a whole-tree build wouldn't actually fix the root cause (unstaged content would still be baked into the build itself). Falsified both directions before committing: a clean staged change + real unstaged dead code elsewhere now passes (and the unstaged change survives, correctly restored); the same dead code staged still correctly blocks.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

acceptance_criteria: Two concurrent agents editing disjoint files in the same working tree can both commit cleanly via the pre-commit hook without --no-verify, even while the other's changes are uncommitted/unstaged.

title: Task 4: computeMaterialHash
description: Plan Task 4 (revised after falsifiable-gate review — see comment). Exported hash over the material set: (artifact_uri, state, full-observation-set-identity) per artifact, sha256, order-insensitive. Deliberately hashes the FULL observation set (at+type for every observation), not just the latest timestamp — the tick is stateless/re-fetches the whole window every time, so a new observation isn't guaranteed to advance the max timestamp (clock skew, backfill, same-second events). title: Move github.ts from REST to GraphQL: CI check-run visibility + native rate-limit data, configurable
description:
Two related gaps surfaced in conversation, consolidated into one follow-up (not part of the currently-executing generalize-board plan):

1. agent/sources/github.ts is REST-only today (/search/issues + per-PR /reviews and /comments) and never fetches CI check-run status. A GraphQL query shaped roughly like:
   repository(owner, name) { pullRequest(number) { commits(last: N) { nodes { commit { checkSuites(first: N) { nodes { checkRuns(first: 50) { nodes { name, conclusion, status } } } } } } } } }
   would close this gap — failing/pending CI is exactly the kind of "needs_you" signal the lifecycle model already exists to surface, and it's currently invisible.

2. GraphQL's response includes rateLimit { remaining, resetAt, cost } directly — richer than REST's header-based rate limit info, and a better foundation for real backoff than what github.ts currently has. User specifically asked for rate-limit behavior (thresholds/backoff) to be configurable, likely via canonical-hours.toml (the config surface added in the generalize-board plan's Task 5) rather than hardcoded.

Scope for whoever picks this up: (a) design whether this is a full REST->GraphQL migration of github.ts or an additive GraphQL call layered alongside the existing REST calls just for check-runs + rate-limit data; (b) how "configurable" rate-limit behavior actually behaves (a backoff curve? a hard stop threshold? surfaced in canonical-hours.toml's existing [schedule] table or a new one); (c) whether check-run state feeds into the existing hard/soft classification model (a failing required check plausibly deserves needs_you) or is a new signal type. Not designed here — genuinely needs its own brainstorm given it touches classification semantics, not just data-fetching.

title: Adopt GraphQL codegen (zod validation) for github.ts/linear.ts raw responses
description:
Validated live (not theoretical) against real fixtures in a throwaway spike, both connectors:

GitHub: fetched the real published schema (npm @octokit/graphql-schema, no live token needed), wrote the EXACT existing SEARCH_QUERY from agent/lib/sources/github.ts as a .graphql operation file, ran @graphql-codegen/cli + typescript + typescript-operations + graphql-codegen-typescript-validation-schema (schema: zod, withOperationType: true). Generated SearchPrsQuerySchema() successfully validated test/fixtures/github/search_windowed.json (the real existing test fixture).

Linear: same process against Linear's public schema.graphql (raw.githubusercontent.com/linear/linear, no token needed) and the exact ISSUES_QUERY from linear.ts. Generated LinearIssuesQuerySchema() successfully validated a fixture built from linear-source.test.ts's own issueNode()/gqlResponse() helpers.

Real gap this closes: today both sources' RAW GraphQL response types (GqlSearchResponse/GqlPullRequest in github.ts, GqlIssuesResponse/GqlIssue in linear.ts) are plain TS interfaces with ZERO runtime validation -- "(await res.json()) as GqlSearchResponse" is an unchecked cast. The EXISTING hand-written GithubPrRecordSchema/LinearIssueRecordSchema only validate a LATER, flattened/normalized record built from that raw response (repository.owner.login -> repository: string, etc.) -- codegen doesn't replace those, it adds a validation layer one step earlier that doesn't exist today.

Real, mechanically-derived correctness win: GitHub's search() genuinely returns a broader union (App|Discussion|Issue|MarketplaceListing|Organization|PullRequest|Repository|User per the real schema) that the hand-written code silently assumes is always PullRequest-shaped. Codegen surfaces the real union automatically and type-safely.

Real friction found by actually hitting it (not guessed):
1. Custom scalars need TWO separate config maps -- `scalars` (TS types) and `scalarSchemas` (zod validators) -- independently, for both GitHub's (DateTime/URI/GitObjectID/GitRefname/Base64String/Date) and Linear's (DateTime/TimelessDate/JSON/JSONObject) custom scalars. Skip either and it silently degrades to z.any()-style validation, not a hard error.
2. Nested unions need __typename requested in the query itself to get real discrimination (e.g. repository.owner, comments.nodes.author in the GitHub query) -- otherwise codegen faithfully reproduces the query's own ambiguity as a duplicated/degenerate union. Not a tool bug -- fixable by tightening the query, real but small work.
3. Naive full-schema codegen is large: GitHub generated 36,167 lines for one query (231 enum schemas generated, only 7 actually referenced); Linear generated 33,560 lines, same shape. Investigated @graphql-codegen/client-preset as a scoping fix but didn't fully resolve compatibility with the third-party zod-validation plugin in the time spent. Pragmatic call: cloister already commits large generated files as routine convention (src/generated/manifest.ts, cluster.ts, tool-schemas.ts) -- a big generated schema file here is not out of step with this ecosystem, so this is not a blocker, just noted.

Scope for implementation: add @graphql-codegen/cli + typescript + typescript-operations + graphql-codegen-typescript-validation-schema as devDependencies to canonical-hours, .graphql operation files mirroring github.ts's SEARCH_QUERY and linear.ts's ISSUES_QUERY, a Taskfile regen target, wire the generated *QuerySchema() as an additional validation step immediately after fetch() in both sources (before the existing flatten/transform logic), tighten both queries to request __typename on nested unions per finding 2, and real tests proving the generated schema actually rejects a drifted/malformed fixture (not just that it accepts a valid one).

title: Recurring false-positive "Cannot find module" diagnostic right after new source files are created
description:
Observed 3 times independently during SDD execution of docs/superpowers/plans/2026-07-17-pr-board-eve-plan.md (Tasks 1, 2, 3 — possibly a 4th in Task 4, not yet confirmed), and each time a task reviewer independently re-ran `pnpm typecheck`/`pnpm vitest run` directly and got a clean pass, confirming the flagged error was stale/false:

1. Task 1: `scaffold.test.ts:2:42 — Cannot find name 'node:fs'` — real tsc run: clean. `@types/node` was correctly installed and resolved.
2. Task 2: `source.test.ts:8:8 — Cannot find module '../agent/sources/source'` — real tsc run: clean, path was correct.
3. Task 3: `lectio-source.test.ts` — same "Cannot find module" pattern on two imports — real tsc + vitest run: clean, 19/19 passing.
4. Task 4 (in progress at time of filing): `github-source.test.ts:3:30 — Cannot find module '../agent/sources/github'` — not yet independently re-verified by a reviewer at time of filing.

Pattern: this is not `pnpm typecheck`/`vitest` itself reporting the error — it's a separate diagnostics-surfacing layer (likely a background LSP/TS-server watching the workspace) whose module-resolution index appears to lag behind newly-created files by some window, producing a transient false "Cannot find module" that clears once the index catches up. Every real compiler/test-runner invocation has been clean.

Not something fixable in this repo's own code — flagging as an environmental/tooling observation so future contributors (or future SDD runs) don't waste time chasing a phantom bug when they see this pattern, and so it's tracked rather than silently repeated. If this is reproducible outside this specific session, it may be worth reporting upstream to the tool that surfaces these diagnostics.

Close condition: either (a) root-caused and fixed if it turns out to be something in this repo's own tsconfig/tooling setup after all, or (b) reported upstream and this bead closed as "not actionable here, tracked upstream" with a link.

comments.text:
Second, distinct manifestation found during Task 3 (generalize-board plan) — different mechanism, same root category (a diagnostics/indexing layer not reading the live checkout).

This time: type-shape mismatch, not "Cannot find module." Diagnostics flagged test/board.test.ts:143-173 with "Property 'snapshots'/'material_hash' does not exist on type {...}" against a fully-expanded Board type lacking those fields — even though the live agent/lib/board.ts and a fresh `pnpm typecheck` (tsc --noEmit) were both clean at the reviewed commit.

Root cause traced (task reviewer investigation, not speculation): `.eve/dev-runtime/snapshots/` (gitignored, outside tsconfig's `include` scope) holds multiple full source-tree checkpoints written by the `eve` dev/build tool. Several pre-dated this task's edit and contained the OLD board.ts (no snapshots/material_hash). Whatever tool surfaces these diagnostics appears to scan more broadly than tsconfig's include list, resolving `Board`'s type from a stale `.eve` snapshot while reading the live, current test file — producing a type error that doesn't exist in the real build.

Same recommended handling as the original finding: verify independently via a real `tsc`/`vitest` run rather than trusting the diagnostic at face value, especially now that it's confirmed to manifest in at least two different error shapes from the same underlying cause.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

comments.text:
Likely root cause found (user's hypothesis, confirmed): `~/github` is a top-level symlink to `~/remotes` (personal dotfiles convention: `lrwxr-xr-x github -> /Users/jamesgardner/remotes`). Every session this repo was worked in used the symlinked path `/Users/jamesgardner/github/art/canonical-hours` (`pwd -L`), while the real path is `/Users/jamesgardner/remotes/art/canonical-hours` (`pwd -P`) — both resolve to identical files, but as two distinct path spellings.

This plausibly explains the exact observed pattern: `tsc`/`vitest` (proper realpath-following module resolution) were always clean, while some separate diagnostics-surfacing layer (a background LSP/TS-server watching the workspace) intermittently flagged "Cannot find module" specifically right after new files were created — exactly when a symlink-path-vs-realpath cache in that watcher would be most likely to lag (the LSP hasn't yet reconciled that the new file exists at both path spellings).

Not a bug in this repo's tsconfig/code — it's a filesystem-layout interaction between the user's home-directory symlink habit and whatever tool surfaces these diagnostics (never identified precisely; not `tsc`/`vitest` themselves). Practical workaround: prefer the real path (`~/remotes/...`) over the symlinked one (`~/github/...`) when opening/working in this repo, to avoid the dual-path confusion.

Closing per the bead's own stated close condition (b): root-caused, not actionable further in this repo specifically.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

acceptance_criteria: Either root-caused as a real repo-local config issue and fixed (verified by the false-positive no longer appearing on the next file-creation task), or confirmed as an external tooling artifact and closed with that explanation.

title: merge.ts clone-safety regression tests don't actually exercise the bug they guard against
description:
Task 5's fix for the reference-identity bug in foldState's lastOther !== lastVerdict check (now value equality) is correct and verified. But the two "clone safety" regression tests added alongside it (test/merge.test.ts, "bare approval survives JSON round-trip" and "unanswered comment is needs_you even after cloning") don't actually catch a regression: reviewer empirically proved this by reverting the fix in an isolated worktree and re-running just those two tests — both still passed against the reverted (buggy) code.

Root cause: within a single foldState() call, lastOther/lastVerdict are derived from the SAME observations array parameter via .sort()/.filter()/.find() — cloning the array upstream (before the call) doesn't cause these internal derivations to diverge in reference, since they all still point at elements of the one array passed in. The actual bug only manifests if two SEPARATELY-cloned instances of the same logical observation end up at different positions in the array passed to foldState — neither test constructs that.

Currently unreachable via the real pipeline anyway: mergeEvents' dedup Map (keyed on uri|at|author) already guarantees no two objects with matching (at, author) survive into one artifact's observations array before foldState ever sees them. So this is defense-in-depth test coverage, not a live defect — didn't block Task 5's merge.

Close condition: either construct a test that genuinely places two independently-cloned copies of the same (at, type) observation at different array positions before calling foldState (and confirms it fails against a reverted fix), or explicitly document in a comment near the value-equality check that it's defense-in-depth for a path the dedup layer currently makes unreachable.

comments.text:
Fixed. Root cause of why the old tests were toothless: lastOther/lastVerdict are always derived from elements of the SAME sorted array within one foldState call, so cloning the whole input array before the call never causes them to diverge in reference — the JSON round-trip tests could never fail.

Replaced both with one test that constructs two genuinely distinct object instances for the same logical event (a hard review_approved + a same-instant soft duplicate, since lastOther's OTHER_ACTIVITY-membership predicate and lastVerdict's classification==="hard" predicate can select different objects). Falsified per the reviewer's own methodology: reverted isSameObservation to reference-identity (lastOther === lastVerdict), confirmed the new test fails (needs_you instead of resolved), restored the real value-equality fix, confirmed it passes. Also tightened the code comment near the check to describe the real mechanism instead of the inaccurate "survive JSON round-trips" framing. Commit 4247b6a, pushed.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

acceptance_criteria: Either a test exists that fails when the value-equality fix in foldState is reverted to reference-identity, or the code has an explicit comment documenting why the current tests don't (and can't currently) exercise that path given the dedup layer's guarantees.

title: Add notme DPoP verification as an ActionGate option
description: action-gate.ts's sharedSecretGate is a static-secret bearer gate — a leaked MCP_ACTION_TOKEN grants indefinite access. notme (~/remotes/art/notme) ships a DPoP-bound access token flow (RFC 9449, /token endpoint) that's genuine proof-of-possession: a stolen access token is inert without the caller's DPoP private key, which never leaves their process. DPoP doesn't require mTLS/TLS-server support (that's the whole point — ADR-006 built it for exactly environments like eve/Vercel that don't expose raw TLS config), so it's implementable today unlike the bridge-cert mTLS path. Vendor notme's gen/ts/dpop.ts (verifyDPoPToken, self-contained, zero deps beyond Web Crypto/fetch, confirmed portable to Node) and add notmeDpopGate() as an ActionGate alongside sharedSecretGate — pluggable via env, not a breaking migration (notme is 'experimental, not audited' per its own README).

title: Verify server.json against a real cloister registration flow
description: server.json (Task 4 of the mcp-cloister build) has only been validated for shape — test/server-json.test.ts parses and asserts its fields, but it has never actually been fed to `cloister add` or an equivalent registration flow against a running canonical-hours instance. Needs a real end-to-end registration test once a cloister instance is available to test against.

comments.text:
Live-verified against cloister's real `task add`/`cluster:resolve` pipeline (cloister repo at /Users/jamesgardner/remotes/art/cloister, commit 6bf763e). Ran `node --import tsx scripts/cli-add.mjs file:///Users/jamesgardner/remotes/art/canonical-hours/server.json --name canonical-hours` — resolved cleanly, produced a `[[generated_backends]]` entry (input=canonical-hours, handlesPrefix="", claims=[], dynamicTools=true) and logged exactly the predicted fallback message: "no _meta.art.cloister/v1 — using single-backend fallback." Confirms server.json is well-formed and cloister-consumable, and confirms the design's no-groups-fallback claim against real code, not just docs.

Two real gotchas found (not previously documented anywhere, worth remembering for anyone else registering an input this way):
1. `file://` refs must point directly AT the server.json file — a directory ref fails with EISDIR (scripts/resolve-inputs.mjs's file:// handler is a bare readFileSync, no directory/index resolution).
2. `urlBinding`/`serviceBinding` (how cloister would actually reach the running server over HTTP) are NOT auto-derived from server.json's `remotes[]` — they're operator-set fields on the `[inputs.<name>]` cluster.toml block, passed through as-is (scripts/resolve-inputs.mjs:715-716,844-849). So "resolves cleanly" proves the registry-document shape is right; it does NOT prove cloister can actually route a tool call to canonical-hours yet — that needs urlBinding wired + a live canonical-hours instance + an actual tool-call round trip through cloister, still unverified.

Also confirmed cloister's resolve-inputs.mjs never reads `_meta.art.cloister/v1.tenancy` at all — tenancy (external/trusted_tier/shares_workerd_with) is consumed downstream (scripts/cluster-to-toml.mjs, toml-to-cluster.mjs, emit-compose.mjs, the manifest layer), not at the resolve stage. Untested here.

Mutation was local-only and reverted (git checkout cluster.toml cluster.lock.toml) — nothing committed to cloister.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

acceptance_criteria: Manual: run cloister's registration flow (cloister add or equivalent) against server.json pointed at a running canonical-hours instance, confirm it resolves and canonical-hours' tools are callable through cloister.

title: eve dev crashes on boot when LECTIO_URL is unset (connections/lectio.ts url validation)
description:
Real, reported bug: on a fresh machine with only GITHUB_TOKEN set, `task dev` crashed at eve's connection-discovery step with "Expected the connection export 'default' from 'connections/lectio.ts' to match the public eve shape. The 'url' field must be a valid URL." Reproduced live (not guessed) via a real eve dev run with the exact env described.

Root cause: agent/connections/lectio.ts's url: process.env.LECTIO_URL ?? "" passes an empty string when LECTIO_URL is unset. eve validates a connection's url field at discovery/build time -- confirmed by reading node_modules/eve/dist/src/public/definitions/connections/mcp.js (no validation there; auth-only) and node_modules/eve/docs/connections/mcp.mdx (every documented example uses a real URL). The actual rejection happens at a different, earlier eve layer than defineMcpClientConnection itself. This is a DIFFERENT validation point than the deterministic tick's own LectioSource (agent/lib/tick-entry.ts), whose "empty-string fallback, degrade lazily inside fetch()" convention does NOT apply here since eve's own connection registration runs at build/discovery time, not lazily -- the earlier "lectio-optional" work (this session) fixed the tick's wiring but missed this separate eve-connection file entirely.

Fixed with a small, deliberately reusable helper (not a one-off patch): agent/lib/optional-connection-url.ts's optionalConnectionUrl(configured) substitutes a syntactically-valid, never-real placeholder ("http://localhost:1/not-configured") when unconfigured, so eve's registration-time shape check passes; an agent that actually tries to call a tool on an unconfigured connection gets a normal connection-refused tool error, not a boot crash. Wired into agent/connections/lectio.ts. Reusable for any future optional eve connection this repo adds.

Verified live end-to-end: reproduced the crash with the exact reported repro (only GITHUB_TOKEN set), applied the fix, re-ran eve dev, confirmed clean boot ("[DEV] server listening at http://127.0.0.1:2001/"), not just a passing type/shape check.

Also fixed a related documentation confusion flagged alongside this bug: .env.example and GETTING_STARTED.md's GITHUB_TOKEN comment implied MCP_ACTION_TOKEN and GITHUB_TOKEN's write scope were linked/conditional on each other ("write-scoped too, if you set MCP_ACTION_TOKEN below"). They're actually two independent gates -- MCP_ACTION_TOKEN controls whether a caller may invoke the two mutating tools at all; GITHUB_TOKEN's scope controls whether the tool's own GitHub call succeeds once invoked. Reworded both docs to make the independence explicit and spell out both failure modes (denied vs. a GitHub 403).

3 new tests (optional-connection-url.test.ts), typecheck + full suite (221 tests) pass.

comments.text: Fixed and verified live end-to-end: reproduced the exact crash (only GITHUB_TOKEN set), applied optionalConnectionUrl(), confirmed clean eve dev boot. Also fixed the related MCP_ACTION_TOKEN/GITHUB_TOKEN doc confusion. 221/221 tests pass. Committed 92c86e3.
